one_line_takeaway: A survey mapping the full Process Reward Model loop (data generation, model building, test-time/RL usage) for LLM reasoning, arguing PRMs offer finer-grained, more interpretable, more functional supervision than outcome reward models but at higher cost, weaker generalization, and greater vulnerability to verbosity/length reward hacking.
key_finding: In the authors' own six-aspect comparison, PRMs win on granularity (structural step-level error localization), interpretability, and functionality (guiding tree search and providing both step- and trajectory-level RL signals), but LOSE to ORMs on resource efficiency, generalization, and anti-hacking robustness. Their original PRM800K stability analysis found the standard deviation of token length is 71.7 at the step level versus 50.6 at the trajectory level, which they interpret as step-level signals being noisier and less constrained, making PRMs easier to 'length-hack' by generating verbose but vacuous intermediate steps. ORMs, grounded in ground-truth outcome verification, are argued to be the most resistant to reward hacking; rule-based rewards are the cheapest but most brittle and prone to exploitation if rules are mis-specified.
core_contribution: A systematic survey of Process Reward Models (PRMs) for LLM reasoning, organized around a 'data-model-usage' closed loop: (1) how to generate process supervision data (human annotation vs. automated vs. semi-automated/hybrid), (2) how to build PRMs (discriminative, generative, implicit, and other architectures), and (3) how to use PRMs (test-time scaling and PRM-guided reinforcement learning). The authors also contribute a six-aspect comparative framework (resource efficiency, granularity, anti-hacking robustness, generalization, interpretability, functionality) that ranks Rule-Based Rewards vs. Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) vs. PRMs, plus a small original stability analysis on the PRM800K dataset. The paper positions itself as distinct from prior surveys (test-time scaling taxonomies, broad reward-modeling taxonomies, generic deep-RL reward design) by uniquely targeting step-level process supervision across the full loop. It is accompanied by an actively maintained GitHub repository.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a reasoning-alignment survey, not a security paper; it does not define an adversary, attack surface, or defense. The nearest security-adjacent content is a discussion of 'reward hacking' as a robustness/alignment concern (see key_finding), but the paper frames this as a reward-signal-quality issue in RL training, not as an adversarial threat model, so none is invented here.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) the data-model-usage taxonomy simplifies/abstracts hybrid methods that span multiple categories, which are filed only under a primary aspect; (2) benchmark and application coverage is selective and representative rather than comprehensive, with no guarantee of completeness for task-specific or proprietary evaluation suites. Reviewer-observed: the only original empirical evidence is a single-dataset (PRM800K) token-length variance statistic used to support a broad reward-hacking claim; the six-aspect Rule/ORM/PRM comparison (Figure 2) is presented as High/Moderate/Low qualitative ratings rather than measured benchmark results, so several comparative conclusions are argumentative rather than empirically quantified.

datasets_used: PRM800K (Lightman et al. 2023) - used for the authors' own step-level vs. trajectory-level token-length stability analysis; also cited as the canonical human-annotated process-supervision dataset
ProcessBench (cited benchmark; competition-level math, earliest-error detection, avg 7.1 steps)
PRMBench (cited benchmark; >6,000 problems, 80,000 step annotations, avg 13.4 steps)
Socratic-PRMBench (cited benchmark; ~3,000 flawed trajectories over six error patterns)
ViLBench / VisualProcessBench / MPBench (cited multimodal PRM benchmarks)
WebRewardBench (cited; WebPRM Collection with 40,000 step-level preference pairs)
quantitative_results: PRM800K stability analysis (authors' own): step-level token-length SD = 71.7 vs. trajectory-level SD = 50.6, interpreted as step-level signals being noisier and more susceptible to length/verbosity reward hacking
Average steps per problem cited to argue PRM annotation cost: ProcessBench = 7.1 steps, PRMBench = 13.4 steps (implying PRM annotation workload is several times higher than ORM)

Cited benchmark scales: PRMBench = >6,000 problems / 80,000 step annotations; Socratic-PRMBench = ~3,000 flawed trajectories / 6 error patterns; WebRewardBench = 40,000 step-level preference pairs; MedS3 = ~8,000 human-curated seed examples
baselines_compared: Rule-Based Reward vs. Outcome Reward Model (ORM) vs. Process Reward Model (PRM) - the authors' six-aspect qualitative comparison (Figure 2) across Resource Efficiency, Granularity, Anti-Hacking Robustness, Generalization, Interpretability, Functionality (High/Moderate/Low ratings)
Within PRM building, the survey contrasts four paradigm classes: Discriminative PRMs, Generative PRMs, Implicit PRMs, and Other Architectural innovations
No head-to-head numeric benchmark results are produced by the authors; comparisons are taxonomic/qualitative plus the single PRM800K variance statistic
